sentences.text: No.
Really?
Really?

Going back to Gunskirchen for one more moment, another horrible sight that I don't believe I'll live long enough to forget was that one day, the three of us were walking-- saw a corpse where the buttock was cut out.
And we saw a guy running with the piece of flesh.
Actually, there we saw cannibalism for the first and only time.
So it only shows that human beings are not much higher than animals when it comes down to instinct.
Yes.
I grabbed bread, and I was walking out.
[LAUGHS] And as soon as I got to the door, somebody grabbed [LAUGHS] the bread out of my hand.
So I went back.
Going back again was risking your life because the horde was pressing.
So I became a little smarter, and broke the bread in half, and put it in the bottom of my pants, and walked out with it.
And finally-- oh, and a piece of-- there were blocks of this fat in the blocks, frozen.
And [LAUGHS] on my other-- and we had a feast.
Of course, the next morning we had a diarrhea that nobody can describe.
Yes.
It was a jeep that I have seen.
There were people who saw them earlier, some others.
And a very tall guy was yelling, screaming in Hungarian that stay put.
Don't move.
We're going to bring you food.
And nobody paid attention.
And they were running like a horde of cattle.
He was shooting his pistol in the air.
And again, he seemed, to me, 10 feet tall, standing on the jeep.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My uncle that I was together with from the first labor on, he actually-- we couldn't stop his diarrhea.
Because the Americans came in without any preparation of-- they did not know how to handle it.
What they did, they did bring in food, but also some canned whatever it was, which was not fit for these empty stomachs that were not used to food anymore.
And people were dying. 10 times as many as before, from this eating.
We were trying to take control, especially this younger uncle of mine who came to last.
And he saw the diarrhea, so we had nothing.
No medication.
He took wood and burned it because he knew, if you burned it crisp, it will sort of stop diarrhea.
And we ate that, but for my other uncle, nothing helped.
So there was nothing else to do, but called the Americans because he had not much confidence in them.
We saw how they handled the sick.
Not because they did not try to, but they had no experience whatsoever, and no medication, no facilities.
They were putting those who wanted to be evacuated to a hospital on a flat bed truck and carried them away.
And we never saw them.
So we put up my uncle.
My other uncle wanted to go with him.
They did not let him.
And that was the last we saw of him.
Which uncle?
Ferenc?
Ferenc.
The one who was with me.
So again, the irony of he survives and then dies of diarrhea or whatever.
After that, finally, a few days later, I got into the typhoid fever.
And my uncle, the remaining one, the younger one, this time, he just did not let himself be thrown away-- thrown off from the truck.
He came with me and they took me to Horsching.
That was the name of it.
It was a police school before.
And that is what became an infirmary for the survivors.
In Austria.
This is all in Austria.
Everything.
Mauthausen on-- everything was in Austria.
In Gunskirchen in Austria.
Near Linz and Wels.
Yes.
I was in the hospital.
And in the hospital, my uncle was looking after me because the doctors and the nurses were all Germans.
Germans.
They were under American supervision.
They probably tried their best, but still, my uncle did not trust them.
He was looking over me like a hawk.
His name is [INAUDIBLE] Moshe-- Moshe.
Moshe.
Yes.
Yes.
In this camp, I remember they put us up on bunk beds, three layers.
And after what I had seen-- as my uncle put me up on the top bunk because the lower ones were throwing up, and whatever, and discharge.
[LAUGHS] The one under.
And for the 24 hours, I slept about 20 hours.
And I was, in my dream, always home, being Friday night or, when I was lucky, a Seder night at home.
And I was there for five or six weeks.
And with my uncle staying with me.
He could have gone home, but he didn't.
And this is it.
Well, that picture is after I had arrived in Hungary.
It was made in Hungary already.
It was probably in mid July or so of 1945.
Yes.
Then they came-- the Bricha, the-- the-- this Palestinian group.
And they wanted, especially the young ones, to persuade us to go to Palestine.
But I had an obsession that I have to go home because my parents are there.
[SOBBING] I saw whatever happened, but it could not happen to my parents.
They will be waiting for me.
And my uncle sort of told me, we should go, too.
I wouldn't hear of it.
So we wanted to go back to Hungary.
They put us on a train, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but I have to go back again for one more moment.
On the day of the liberation in Gunskirchen, it was late afternoon.
It was still dusk, but you could still see.
All of a sudden, we saw those Sonderkommando children because they were still in the striped uniform.
They were running into the camp.
One had a [NON-ENGLISH], but her leg was still in it.
A [NON-ENGLISH].
A German [NON-ENGLISH].
But her leg was still in it.
Another one with her torso.
And that was the sergeant from Mauthausen, so that was the only, only [NON-ENGLISH], the revenge that I have seen that was taken.
And also, I should say that the next day, when the Americans came in, they gathered some of the SS-- former SS guards who were hiding in the forests.
They caught them, but they were already in civilian suits.
But they had the tattoo.
You know, the SS had a tattoo on their arms with the death head.
And they gathered them, and they were slapping the corpses into the mass graves, like we used to, before.
And I saw, with my own eyes, that some of the Americans did the same thing.
They kicked them into the grave, like they used to do with us.
Sonderkommando.
Unfortunately, yes.
You know, we did not realize then.
And again, there was no thinking, and no rationalizing, no logic, no rhyme, reason-- only instinct.
We were afraid of them because they were strong.
And if we had any food and they saw it, they grabbed it.
They took it.
But these were people who were dehumanized to the maximum.
They were Polish.
They went back.
I had no contact with them.
OK.
Two episodes.
After we declared that we wanted to go back to Hungary, they put us on a train-- the Americans.
And we went towards Hungary from Austria.
On the way, we started to talk among each other.
Who are you?
Who are you?
Everybody.
And one guy says whatever his name was.
Joe Klein from Debrecen.
Another guy goes, Debrecen?
Yeah, I know a Joe Klein.
Where did you live?
So he gives him a street number.
He looks at him.
He says, you are not that?
Who are you?
It turned out that he was a Hungarian Gendarme who escaped to the West, picked up one of the dead-- after the liberation, they issued some certificates.

And that guy died, and this guy took his certificate, and declared himself to be that guy, and came back to Hungary because he was afraid to go on with his own identity because he was a multiple murderer.
So at that point, they grabbed him, took him out of the train, and under the wheels.
Finished.
This is the extent of what animals we were at that point.
I did have enough strength [LAUGHTER] at that point.
And the other one-- while we were in Horsching, there were next to us, just separated with a chain link fence, another group of people were still in the Hungarian Gendarme uniform.
These Hungarian Gen-- the Gendarme was the same in Hungary as the SS in Germany.
Next to us, Jews.
This is in the hospital where, at that point, I was already walking outside, too, and allowed.
And there was a gate, also, between us that they could open from their side.
And they came in, at will, to our side.
And they locked it.

And one morning, one of the rabbis-- [SIGHS] because even then, people were dropping like flies, dead-- and the rabbi said, OK, we should make a minyan and say at least a Kaddish out in the open area.
And then he shot in the air.
And he said, if any of you ever open your mouth, I will personally kill you.
So this was the other episode that I wanted to say, but this, again, was still the liberating army, not the occupying.
So when we arrived with this train to [NON-ENGLISH], that was occupied by the Russians.
The Americans had to get off, and the Russians came in.
But by then, we had little things gathered.
The Joint Distribution gave us some clothing.
We had some tchotchkes and little things.
You know, this and that.
And everybody was jealous.
Russians came in, and they said, everybody gets off of this train.
The engine has to get some water, and then we'll bring it back.
So everybody got off.
And leave everything there.
And we arrived to Hungary at night-- middle of the night.
They told us we cannot leave the train station because there's a curfew.
The Russians occupied Budapest.
And whoever got out on the street, they stripped them naked.
[LAUGHS] The Russians were unbelievable in horde.
These were the Mongolians.
This was already the occupying army, not the fighting Russians.
They [LAUGHS] brought in the Mongolians.
And jokes are being told to them, but it is the truth that I was there, personally, when one of them came into a watchmaker, brought in a huge clock, and he said-- they were maniac.
They were-- they were-- obsession with the wrist watches for them.
He brought in a big clock, and he said, make me six wrist watches [LAUGHTER] from this.
Now they're telling it as a joke, but that was a fact.
They raped women from 6 to 70.
[LAUGHS] So this was our introduction to Hungary.
And-- and finally, in the morning, I had an uncle and aunt living in Budapest.
And we went to their house, hoping and still waiting for the good news that our parents-- and we did not know then.
My aunt had a daughter and a son.
The son came back.
And after liberation, died in her hands.
They say it now that it was meningitis that was rampant in Hungary after the war.
And she went berserk, that woman.
She actually went crazy.
So when we arrived, that was a few days after they buried her son.
When we arrived and they saw us, she started to scream, your mother, Margit, is dead.
Your father, Simon, is dead.
Nobody's alive.
My son is dead.
Everybody is dead.
So I thought I went nuts, too.
Crazy.
And this was the introduction to the welcome home to Hungary.
[SIGHS] And we stayed in Hungary for one year.
I stayed in Budapest for six weeks or so.
I was still not very strong.
Later.
But while I was in Budapest, that oldest brother of mine who was liberated in Mauthausen, he came home with a heart problem because of the high fever.
We learned it later that his valves were gone.
And he lived until '86, but, off and on, he was in the hospitals.
He was in one hospital.
In Budapest.
In this-- Budapest.
He never left Hungary after he went back.
And after that, I went back to Gyongyos.
I went, and to tell you the truth, at that time, I did not have much faith in God.
After this aunt episode, then I said, oh, I don't want to have anything to do.
Whatever I believed is-- is gone.
If there is a God, he's impotent or very cruel.
So I don't want anything to do with it.

And yet, when I went down to Gyongyos, I said, about me, I do whatever I want, but I have to go to the shul and say a Kaddish for my parents because that, I [? wanted ?] So I wanted to go to the shul.
And the orthodox shul in Gyongyos was on a little hill.
And there were some 12, 14 steps to go up on the hill.
And as I opened the door to the shul, I am faced with the behinds of horses.
These Russian son of a guns, they did it on purpose to denigrate any-- but they did that with the churches, too, I have to admit.
And you know how uncomfortable to schlep up horses on a hill, on steps?
And they did it just to the show how much think-- so I went into that shul.
[SIGHS]
And where the sacred Torahs used to be kept, there was a huge manure-- bunch of manure.
Horses were defecating, urinating where my Rabbi Yungrais used to daven.
I had enough of it there, and I said, never ever again I want to believe in God because there isn't any.
Can't help it.
When the children come, what can you tell them?
When I first came here, one of the reasons that I changed my name was that I wanted to get away from it all.
OK.
So in Gyongyos, I came back.
One of the main purposes was that I wanted to find somebody who can tell me something concrete about my family.
Not much after, I found out about my brother.
I met that person whom he nurtured back to life and he left him.
And I spat him in his face.
And I told him what I think of him.
He-- he did not tell me.
Somebody else told me about it.
But he did not deny it.
He said, I had to come home.
I wanted to meet up with my family.
Oh, yeah.
He was one of his buddies.
So and then I was asking and looking for somebody who knew something about my parents.
And they told me that my mother was last seen going with a girl named Martha Kahan.
And I knew her very well.
And she told me that, from the ghetto, her mother was caught in Budapest when the Germans came in and sent right away into a-- she was interned, right then and there.
Much earlier than the ghetto was even established in Gyongyos.
So she was left alone with her father.
And in the ghetto, after I left, my mother sort of took her under her arm because she was a young girl, alone.
And they went in the same wagon and on the train to Auschwitz.
And in Auschwitz, they went in front of Mengele together.
And Martha was sent to the right and my mother to the left.
So she was an eye witness that my mother died.
[CRYING] And I married that girl.
She was my first wife.
In 1946, October, we left Hungary because we did not feel that we could live there.
Oh, we escaped on a Russian truck.
We bribed the driver, who had to go anyhow to Austria.
And he took us over to the border.
No.
No.
Her brother, myself, and a bunch of other people went over to Austria.
'46 October.
Yes.
Same age.
Same age.
We were born in the same year.
Yes.
We were in Vienna for awhile.
We were in the Rothschild Hospital for a week.
And then we went to Salzburg and then to Stuttgart, near Stuttgart, in Schwabisch Hall.
And we were there and in Ulm for three years.
And exactly three years later, in October 1949, we arrived to the United States.
First, in Austria, in Salzburg.
And then in Germany, in Schwabisch Hall.
And in Germany, in Ulm.
All DP camps.
In the DP camp, first, we went to study radio technique, radio installation, and whatnot.
And my late wife, she's-- she went to-- these are all true [? art ?] schools.
She did shirt making.
And then we all thought that within six months, we would all be in America.
We had affidavits.
What more [LAUGHS] can you ask for?
And it dragged out for three years.
And then we both worked in the UNRRA first.
UNRRA.
It was administrating the DP camps.
And in 1948, I believe, the IRO took over from the UNRRA.
International Refugee Organization.
And in the meantime, of course, while we were in Germany, there was the establishment of the state of Israel.
And at this point, our whole life, after the war, was aimless, useless.
We felt that this-- how can you live with these memories?
How can you live a normal life being alone?
Not to have a parent, brothers-- once we counted, we lost just grandparents, uncles, aunts, cousins.
Some 87 of them.
So-- [SIGHS] And this went on until 1948.
When the state of Israel was established, it gave us a new life.
We felt, even though the Arabs [LAUGHS] or the Israeli Jews don't like to hear it, from the ashes of the 6 million, Israel was established.
Then, somehow, we accepted the fact that there is some justice in this world.
That something came out from this horrible tragedy of the humankind.
After 2,000 years, we have a land, a country.
And had there been one in the '30s, in the early '40s, who knows how many more Jews would be alive today.
Yes.
Yes, we did.
We sent our belongings to Israel, bec-- frankly, not because we wanted so much to go, but because it was so hopeless to wait for America, it seemed, at one time.
But after, again, because we became complacent, that, oh, we suffered enough, we want to live [LAUGHS] a little-- I should say that one of my brother in-laws went to Palestine in '46, '47.
And he kept on writing us to the camp-- don't come here.
Life is very, very difficult.
And so on and so on.
But when it seemed, after four years, that we can't make it, we sent our belongings.
[LAUGHS] And yet, we stayed behind.
So-- but after the establishment of the state of Israel, even though we did not go to Israel we felt that something is worthwhile to continue to live.
If, for nothing else, to help Israel.
And again--
